items.title: k-NN fraud scoring with Minkowski distance
items.scenario: An analyst builds k-NN on transaction feature vectors (amount z-scored, merchant category one-hot, velocity stats). They try `metric='minkowski', p=1` vs `p=2` in cross-validation: Manhattan slightly wins because a few sparse categorical dimensions create axis-aligned separation.
items.explanation: Minkowski with p=1 treats each feature gap equally in absolute terms; p=2 squares large gaps on continuous velocity features. The winning p reflects which geometry matches fraudulent vs normal transaction clusters.

items.title: RAG passage retrieval with cosine similarity
items.scenario: A support bot embeds user questions and knowledge-base chunks into 384-d vectors. The vector store returns top-5 passages by cosine similarity to the query embedding, ignoring chunk token count.
items.explanation: Long and short passages can have different L2 norms but similar semantic direction; cosine prevents length from dominating retrieval rankings.

items.title: k-means customer segmentation
items.scenario: A marketing team clusters customers using k-means on standardized RFM features (recency, frequency, monetary value). Centroids update by mean coordinates under squared Euclidean distance.
items.explanation: k-means objective is tied to Euclidean geometry; switching to Manhattan would require a different algorithm (e.g. k-medians). Scaling ensures recency and monetary value contribute comparably.
